mappings.rationaleAI: The Enlightenment covers the intellectual origins and spirit relevant to science in the age of modern revolutions (A.5.b), institutional organization of science under the French Revolution (A.5.c), and the Empiricist/Rationalist/Kantian epistemological debates (B.2.c).

History of science directly maps to the entire section A across all periods: ancient/medieval (A.2), other civilizations including Islamic science (A.3 — priority), early modern (A.4), industrial era (A.5), 19th century (A.6), 20th century (A.7), and contemporary issues (A.8).

The overarching article on science covers its history (A broadly), the nature and scope of science and philosophy of science (B.1), elements of the scientific enterprise (B.3), and the social relevance of science (B.6).

The Renaissance directly corresponds to European science in the early modern period (A.4.a), covering the state of science in early 15th-century Europe (A.4.a.i), influences of technology and exploration (A.4.a.ii), and coexistence of new discoveries with old philosophy (A.4.a.iii).

The Scientific Revolution directly matches A.4.b (revolution in natural philosophy), covering the radical reformulation of natural knowledge by Bacon, Descartes, and Galileo (A.4.b.i) and the establishment of scientific societies and progress (A.4.b.ii).

The Islamic Golden Age directly addresses the priority path A.3 (science in other civilizations: Islamic science), covering mathematics, astronomy, physics, chemistry, and medicine across the Abbasid caliphate era.

Traces mathematical-scientific development across Greek civilization (A.2.a), Islamic culture (A.3 — priority path), early modern Europe (A.4), and 19th-century rigor (A.6), providing cross-period and cross-civilizational coverage.

Galileo is explicitly named in A.4.b.i as a central figure in the revolution in natural philosophy; his experimental method also exemplifies empirical procedures of science (B.3.b) and the nature of scientific discovery (B.4.a).
Aristotle represents the Aristotelian view of nature contrasted with Platonism (A.2.a.ii) and is central to the classical and medieval development of the philosophy of science (B.2.a).

Newton's Principia was the culminating result of the new natural philosophy (A.4.b.ii); his synthesis defined the framework debated in Enlightenment epistemology (B.2.c) and assumed uniquely rational through the early 19th century (B.2.d).

Lavoisier's chemical revolution exemplifies science during the Industrial Revolution (A.5.a), occurred in the French Revolutionary institutional context (A.5.c), and marks 18th-century progress in chemistry (A.6).

Faraday's experimental discoveries in electromagnetism belong to science in the Industrial Revolution (A.5.a) and 19th-century progress in physics (A.6), demonstrating the characteristic experimental style of that era.

Gödel's incompleteness theorems are a key development of early 20th-century science (A.7), a response to the 20th-century philosophical debate including logical positivism (B.2.e), and set permanent limits on the scope of scientific formalism (B.4.a).

Medicine's history spans all major periods — Greek humoral theory (A.2.a), Islamic preservation (A.3 — priority path), early modern anatomy (A.4), 19th-century germ theory (A.6), and 20th-century developments (A.7) — providing substantive cross-period coverage.

Descartes is explicitly named in A.4.b.i as a founder of the revolution in natural philosophy; his deductive approach also defines one side of the Baconian/Cartesian methodological debate in 17th-century philosophy of science (B.2.b).

The scientific method directly covers the philosophy of science's nature and scope (B.1), elements of the scientific enterprise (B.3 — empirical, formal, conceptual), and movements of thought including discovery (B.4.a) and justification (B.4.b).

Copernicus's heliocentric model initiated the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b) and exemplifies the coexistence of new discoveries with old philosophical views in the Renaissance period (A.4.a.iii).

Avicenna is a central figure of Islamic Golden Age science — covering medicine, natural science, mathematics, and philosophy — directly addressing the priority path A.3 (science in other civilizations: Islamic science).

Reason as a philosophical concept underlies the 17th-century Baconian vs. Cartesian methodological debate (B.2.b), scientific discovery processes (B.4.a), and the philosophical status of scientific concepts and propositions (B.5.a).

Epistemology directly addresses the nature and scope of the philosophy of science (B.1), the philosophical status of scientific propositions (B.5.a), and connects historical philosophical approaches (B.2) to scientific knowledge and its limits.

Western philosophy traces the intellectual traditions that directly shaped the philosophy of science across all historical sub-periods: classical (B.2.a), 17th century (B.2.b), 18th century (B.2.c), 19th century (B.2.d), and 20th century (B.2.e).

History of philosophy covers the intellectual traditions — from Aristotle through Empiricism, Rationalism, and Kantianism — that form the historical development of the philosophy of science (B.2 and its sub-paths A through C).

Ancient Greece directly covers pre-Periclean protoscience (A.2.a.i), development of scientific attitudes and Platonic vs. Aristotelian views (A.2.a.ii), and the Hellenistic age with Alexandria as the foremost research centre (A.2.a.iii).

The Age of Discovery connects to the influence of exploration and trade demands placed upon science in the Renaissance (A.4.a.ii), driving advances in navigation, cartography, and observational astronomy.

Romanticism corresponds directly to A.5.d (Romantic reaction and science: the proponents of Naturphilosophie) as the cultural and intellectual movement reacting against Enlightenment rationalism and mechanistic science.

Human history provides contextual coverage across all major periods in the history of science (A) — ancient civilizations, Islamic Golden Age (A.3), early modern period (A.4), and Industrial Revolution (A.5) — though as secondary context rather than primary focus.
The early modern period directly covers the European Renaissance (A.4.a), the revolution in natural philosophy (A.4.b), and characteristics of European science (A.4.c) as primary historical content.

Turing's work on computability is a key development of 20th-century science (A.7) and exemplifies the formal structures and limits of scientific inference — the decidability problem as a limit on formal analysis (B.3.c).

Ibn Khaldun was an important scholar of the medieval Islamic world whose Muqaddimah introduced systematic empirical social analysis, contributing to the tradition of Islamic intellectual production covered by A.3 (priority path).

History of technology connects to the influence of technology on science in the Renaissance (A.4.a.ii), science during the Industrial Revolution (A.5.a), and 19th-century scientific progress driven by technological change (A.6).
Sociology provides the disciplinary basis for understanding the social significance of science and the sociology of knowledge that shapes scientific institutions and practice (B.6).

Physics provides the key case studies for 19th-century scientific progress (A.6), early 20th-century transformation via relativity and quantum mechanics (A.7), and the 20th-century philosophical debate responding to these changes (B.2.e).

Mathematics as the formal language of science connects to Greek science (A.2.a), Islamic mathematical contributions (A.3 — priority), the mathematization of nature in the Scientific Revolution (A.4.b.i), and formal structures of scientific inference (B.3.c).

Leonardo epitomizes the Renaissance integration of systematic observation with natural philosophy (A.4.a), bridging artistic practice and early scientific methodology under the influence of technology and exploration (A.4.a.ii) while working within the coexistence of old and new ideas (A.4.a.iii).
